title: Sweet Poison: The Spirit of Sugar and the Slave Trade
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain why rum carries a heavy history and how this episode studies it.
Subject: Episode introduction | Run time: about 3 minutes

[WELCOME] Welcome back to Better Vice Club, Season 2, The Oldest Toast. This is Anthony McDonald. We have studied beer, wine, and whiskey. Today we come to rum, and this is the hardest episode of the season, because rum cannot be told honestly without telling the story of the Atlantic slave trade.

[HOOK] The honest note matters more than ever here. This is a history class, not a tasting room, and rum's history is full of real human suffering that we will not look away from. The hook is this: rum was born from garbage. It began as a way to make money from molasses, the sticky waste left over from refining sugar. And the people who first made it, and whose forced labor made the whole system run, were enslaved Africans.

[TEACH] We read rum through four lenses. Geography asks why the Caribbean grew the sugar, how each island makes a different rum, and how the brutal triangle of the Atlantic trade was drawn on the map. Social studies tells the story of the Haitian Revolution, the only successful large-scale slave revolt, and the crushing debt that followed, and the Maroons who freed themselves. Economics asks who got paid then, who gets paid now, and how one island fought back. English language arts looks at how a Caribbean writer reclaimed this history, and how tiki bars tried to erase it. Each lesson is short and ends with a quick question.

[REMEMBER] Hold this. Rum is the spirit of sugar, and sugar in the colonial Caribbean was built on the forced labor of enslaved people. To drink in the history of rum honestly is to hold both the craft and the cruelty at once. That is why this episode is called Sweet Poison.

[CHECK] Quick check. What waste product is rum originally made from, and whose labor produced it? [Beat] Molasses, the byproduct of refining sugar, produced by the forced labor of enslaved Africans on Caribbean plantations.

## Key Takeaways
- Rum is the spirit of sugar, first made from molasses, the waste left over from refining sugar.
- Rum's history is inseparable from the Atlantic slave trade and the forced labor of enslaved Africans.
- This episode is educational and honest about that history and about alcohol's harms; it is not an endorsement of drinking.
- Better Vice Club reads rum through geography, social studies, economics, and English language arts.

## Sources
- Smith, F. H. (2005). Caribbean rum: A social and economic history. University Press of Florida.

title: Why the Caribbean
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain what sugarcane needs to grow and how that geography was used to justify forced labor.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we opened the rum episode. Two quick questions.
One: what waste product is rum first made from? [Beat] Molasses, the byproduct of refining sugar (Smith, 2005).
Two: whose forced labor produced it? [Beat] Enslaved Africans on Caribbean plantations (Smith, 2005).

[HOOK] Sugarcane is a demanding plant, and it will only thrive in a narrow set of conditions. The Caribbean happens to match all of them. But that match did more than grow sugar. It became an argument that powerful people used to justify enslaving millions.

[TEACH] Sugarcane is Saccharum officinarum, and it wants heat, water, and sun (Galloway, 1989). It needs temperatures of roughly 70 to 95 degrees Fahrenheit all year, about 50 to 80 inches of rain, rich and well-drained soil, twelve or more hours of sunlight in the growing season, and absolutely no frost. The Caribbean archipelago runs from about 10 degrees North at Trinidad to about 26 degrees North at the Bahamas, sitting almost perfectly inside that zone. The volcanic islands add ideal volcanic loam. So the Caribbean is, by climate and soil, close to perfect for cane.
Here is the part that matters most, and it is uncomfortable. The Caribbean's fitness for sugarcane did not simply create the plantation system by accident. Colonial planners used that geography as an argument. Because the land could grow enormous wealth in sugar, and because the work of cutting and processing cane was brutal and deadly, they claimed it justified importing enslaved people to do it. Geography was turned into economic logic, and that logic was used to defend forced labor (Smith, 2005). The map did not cause slavery. People chose slavery, and used the map to excuse it.

[PICTURE] Picture the long arc of islands curving from South America up toward Florida, all of them bathed in steady tropical heat and rain. Now picture how a 1600s European investor looked at that arc: not as home to anyone, but as a machine for making sugar, and a reason, in his mind, to fill it with people taken by force.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the Caribbean's tropical climate and volcanic soil made it close to perfect for sugarcane, and colonial powers used that geographic advantage as an argument to justify enslaving millions of Africans to work it (Galloway, 1989; Smith, 2005). Geography was weaponized.

[CHECK] Quick check. How was the Caribbean's suitability for sugarcane used by colonial powers? [Beat] As an argument to justify importing and enslaving Africans to do the brutal work of growing and processing cane (Smith, 2005).

## Key Takeaways
- Sugarcane (Saccharum officinarum) needs year-round heat, heavy rain, rich soil, strong sun, and no frost (Galloway, 1989).
- The Caribbean, from about 10 to 26 degrees North with volcanic soil, matches those needs almost perfectly.
- Colonial planners used that geographic fitness as an argument to justify forced labor.
- The geography did not cause slavery; people chose it and used the map to excuse it.

## Sources
- Galloway, J. H. (1989). The sugar cane industry: An historical geography from its origins to 1914. Cambridge University Press.
- Smith, F. H. (2005). Caribbean rum: A social and economic history. University Press of Florida.

title: Different Islands, Different Rums
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how geography and tradition make each island's rum distinct.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we learned why the Caribbean grew sugar. Two quick questions.
One: name two things sugarcane needs. [Beat] Any two of year-round heat, heavy rain, rich soil, strong sun, no frost (Galloway, 1989).
Two: how was that geography used by colonial powers? [Beat] As an argument to justify forced labor (Smith, 2005).

[HOOK] Taste a Jamaican rum next to a Cuban rum and you would not guess they are the same kind of drink. One is heavy, funky, and wild; the other is light, dry, and clean. Same crop, same region, completely different spirits. This is terroir again, the taste of place, this time in cane.

[TEACH] Rum splits first by what it is made from (Smith, 2005). Most rum is molasses rum, made from that sugar-refining byproduct, and its character comes mostly from how each distillery ferments and distills. But rhum agricole (ROOM ah-gree-KOHL) is made from fresh-pressed cane juice, so it captures the season and soil of a specific island, true terroir. Martinique is the home of that style, grassy and vegetal.
Then rum splits by island tradition. Jamaica, hot and humid with volcanic soil, makes heavy pot-still rum loaded with esters, the fruity, funky compounds, and it is built for long aging. Jamaican distillers even add dunder (DUN-der), the leftover liquid from a previous distillation, to deepen the funk, a microbial signature tied to one place. Barbados, the birthplace of rum, sits on coral limestone and makes a balanced, elegant style. Guyana's Demerara rum, made in rare wooden pot stills, is dark and heavy with prune and tobacco. And Cuba and Puerto Rico, using column stills, make light, clean rum built for cocktails, the Bacardi model.
So rum is a map you can taste. The raw material, the still, the climate, and the local tradition all leave a fingerprint, and no two islands leave the same one.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Each island is a different flavor. Funky, high-ester Jamaica. Elegant Barbados. Grassy agricole Martinique. Dark Demerara Guyana. Light, mixable Cuba and Puerto Rico. One sea, many spirits.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: rum's style depends on its raw material and its island, so molasses rum reflects distillery tradition while rhum agricole from fresh cane juice reflects true terroir, and Jamaica's funky pot-still rum differs sharply from Cuba's light column-still rum (Smith, 2005). Place is in the bottle.

[CHECK] Quick check. What is the difference between molasses rum and rhum agricole? [Beat] Molasses rum is made from the sugar byproduct and reflects distillery tradition; rhum agricole is made from fresh cane juice and reflects the terroir of its island (Smith, 2005).

## Key Takeaways
- Molasses rum is made from the sugar byproduct; rhum agricole is made from fresh cane juice and shows true terroir (Smith, 2005).
- Jamaica makes heavy, funky, high-ester pot-still rum, sometimes deepened with dunder.
- Barbados, the birthplace of rum, makes a balanced style; Guyana's Demerara rum is dark and heavy.
- Cuba and Puerto Rico make light column-still rum for cocktails, so each island leaves its own fingerprint.

## Sources
- Smith, F. H. (2005). Caribbean rum: A social and economic history. University Press of Florida.

title: The Triangular Trade
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can describe the three legs of the Atlantic triangular trade and rum's place in it.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we tasted the islands. Two quick questions.
One: what is rhum agricole made from? [Beat] Fresh-pressed cane juice (Smith, 2005).
Two: which island makes heavy, funky, high-ester rum? [Beat] Jamaica (Smith, 2005).

[HOOK] On a map, the Atlantic slave trade made a triangle. Three legs, three regions, one engine of profit and cruelty. And rum sat in two of those legs at once, both a product of the trade and a currency used to fuel it.

[TEACH] The triangle had three legs (Slave Voyages Database, 2024). On the first leg, ships carried manufactured goods from New England and Europe, rum, textiles, metalwork, and weapons, to the West African coast, where they were exchanged for captured people. On the second leg, the Middle Passage, those enslaved Africans were carried across the Atlantic to the Caribbean and the Americas. On the third leg, ships carried the products of plantation labor, sugar, molasses, and rum, back to consumers in New England and Europe. Then the circuit began again.
Hold the scale of the Middle Passage honestly. According to the Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database, about 12.5 million Africans were enslaved and shipped across the Atlantic between 1500 and 1900, and roughly 1.8 million of them died during the crossing, a mortality of about 14 percent (Slave Voyages Database, 2024). The Caribbean alone received about 4.7 million people.
Now see rum's double role. Rum was distilled in the Caribbean and in New England by the hundreds of distilleries, and it was carried to Africa as a trade good to buy more enslaved people. So rum was made by enslaved labor, and rum helped pay for enslaving more (Smith, 2005). The same drink sat on both the cruel beginning and the profitable end of the circuit.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Trace the three lines. New England to the African coast carrying rum and guns. The African coast to Barbados carrying people in chains. Barbados back to New England carrying sugar and rum. A closed loop, turning human beings into profit, over and over for centuries.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the triangular trade had three legs, goods to Africa, enslaved people across the Middle Passage, and sugar and rum back to consumers, and about 12.5 million Africans were trafficked, with rum serving both as a product of slavery and as currency to buy more enslaved people (Slave Voyages Database, 2024; Smith, 2005). Rum sat on two legs of the triangle.

[CHECK] Quick check. What were the three legs of the triangular trade? [Beat] Goods like rum and weapons to West Africa, enslaved people across the Middle Passage to the Caribbean, and sugar, molasses, and rum back to Europe and New England (Slave Voyages Database, 2024).

## Key Takeaways
- The triangular trade ran goods to Africa, enslaved people across the Middle Passage, and sugar and rum back to consumers (Slave Voyages Database, 2024).
- About 12.5 million Africans were trafficked between 1500 and 1900, and roughly 1.8 million died on the crossing.
- Rum was both a product of enslaved labor and a currency used to buy more enslaved people (Smith, 2005).
- The trade formed a closed loop that turned human beings into profit for centuries.

## Sources
- Slave Voyages Database. (2024). Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database. Emory University. https://www.slavevoyages.org/
- Smith, F. H. (2005). Caribbean rum: A social and economic history. University Press of Florida.

title: The Caribbean Today
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain the geographic irony linking the Caribbean's colonial wealth to its present challenges.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 5 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we mapped the triangular trade. Two quick questions.
One: roughly how many Africans were trafficked across the Atlantic? [Beat] About 12.5 million between 1500 and 1900 (Slave Voyages Database, 2024).
Two: what were rum's two roles in the trade? [Beat] A product of enslaved labor and a currency to buy more enslaved people (Smith, 2005).

[HOOK] Here is a hard geographic irony. The Caribbean islands that were the most valuable possessions on Earth in the 1700s are today among the most economically challenged nations in the Western Hemisphere. That is not bad luck. It follows directly from how they were used.

[TEACH] Think about what a colonial plantation economy did (Beckford & Campbell, 2013). It extracted wealth, sugar, molasses, and rum, and shipped it out to enrich faraway owners. It did not build local infrastructure, schools, or locally owned industry, because the islands were treated as machines for export, not as homes for people with futures. So when the colonial era ended, the islands were left with the damage of extraction and little of the wealth they had produced. The poverty was structured into the system from the start.
Now add the present danger. The same geography that made the Caribbean perfect for sugarcane, its tropical, low-lying, ocean-surrounded position, makes it one of the most climate-vulnerable regions in the world. Warming oceans are making hurricanes more intense. Sea level rise threatens low coastal land. Coral bleaching hurts tourism and fishing. Shifting rainfall stresses the very cane that started the story (Beckford & Campbell, 2013). The features that drew the colonizers now expose the islands to the climate crisis they did the least to cause.

[PICTURE] Picture one island twice. In 1750, it is the richest place an empire owns, its hills covered in cane, its harbor full of ships. In 2025, it is the same island, still beautiful, but carrying generations of extracted wealth that never came back, bracing for the next stronger hurricane.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the Caribbean's colonial-era plantation wealth was extracted without local reinvestment, leaving structured poverty, and the same tropical geography now makes the region acutely vulnerable to climate change (Beckford & Campbell, 2013). The map that made it valuable now makes it fragile.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why are many Caribbean nations economically challenged today despite their past colonial value? [Beat] Colonial plantation economies extracted wealth without reinvesting in local infrastructure, education, or industry, leaving structured poverty (Beckford & Campbell, 2013).

## Key Takeaways
- Colonial plantation economies extracted Caribbean wealth without reinvesting locally, building poverty into the system (Beckford & Campbell, 2013).
- The islands most valuable to empires are now among the most economically challenged in the hemisphere.
- The same tropical, low-lying geography now makes the Caribbean highly vulnerable to climate change.
- Stronger hurricanes, sea level rise, and shifting rainfall threaten the region that did least to cause the crisis.

## Sources
- Beckford, G. L., & Campbell, M. (2013). Persistent poverty: Underdevelopment in plantation economies of the Third World. University of the West Indies Press.
- Slave Voyages Database. (2024). Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database. Emory University. https://www.slavevoyages.org/

title: The Haitian Revolution
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how enslaved people in Saint-Domingue won the only successful large-scale slave revolution in the Western Hemisphere.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we looked at the Caribbean today. Two quick questions.
One: why are many Caribbean nations economically challenged today despite their past colonial value? [Beat] Colonial plantation economies extracted wealth without reinvesting in local infrastructure, education, or industry, leaving structured poverty (Beckford & Campbell, 2013).
Two: name one climate threat to the region. [Beat] Stronger hurricanes, sea level rise, or shifting rainfall (Beckford & Campbell, 2013).

[HOOK] In 1789, the single most profitable colony on Earth was a place most people today could not find on a map. It was Saint-Domingue, the western part of Hispaniola, the colony that would become Haiti. And the people who made it rich were about to do something no enslaved population had ever done at this scale. They were going to free themselves and keep that freedom.

[TEACH] First, see how much was at stake. In 1789, Saint-Domingue was France's most profitable colonial possession (James, 1989). It produced about 40 percent of Europe's sugar and about 60 percent of its coffee, plus rum, cotton, and indigo (Smith, 2005). All of that wealth rested on forced labor. Roughly 500,000 enslaved Africans lived in the colony, about 65 percent of the whole population (James, 1989). For France, this was the prize colony. For the people working the cane, it was one of the deadliest places in the Atlantic world.
Now the revolution itself. In August 1791, enslaved leaders met at a gathering known as the Bois Caiman ceremony and planned an uprising (James, 1989). On August 22, 1791, the revolt began, and within weeks the northern plantations were in flames (James, 1989). This was not a riot. It was organized political action. The leaders strategized, recruited, negotiated, and fought a long war against one of the strongest militaries in Europe. By 1793, France abolished slavery in the colony, partly to win enslaved soldiers to its side (James, 1989). The fighting went on for years. On January 1, 1804, Haiti declared independence (James, 1989).
Hold onto why this matters. This was the only successful large-scale slave revolution in the history of the Western Hemisphere, and it produced the first Black republic (James, 1989). People that an empire had defined as property organized, took up arms, and built a free nation. The Trinidadian historian C. L. R. James made this his central point. The revolution was not luck and not an accident. It was the logical result of enslaved people taking the words "liberty, equality, and fraternity" seriously, words the colonial powers claimed but did not honor (James, 1989). Treat the people in this story as agents. They made choices, took risks, and changed history.

[PICTURE] Picture the colony in two frames. In the first, 1789, the harbor is packed with ships loading sugar and coffee for France, and the hills are covered in cane worked by half a million people held in bondage. In the second, January 1, 1804, the same land is a free nation, governed by the descendants of those same workers, the first Black republic in the hemisphere.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: in Saint-Domingue, France's richest colony, about 500,000 enslaved Africans launched an uprising in August 1791, won the abolition of slavery there in 1793, and declared independence as Haiti on January 1, 1804, the only successful large-scale slave revolution in the Western Hemisphere and the first Black republic (James, 1989). The people freed themselves.

[CHECK] Quick check. What made the Haitian Revolution historically unique? [Beat] It was the only successful large-scale slave revolution in the Western Hemisphere, and it created the first Black republic, with enslaved people winning and keeping their own freedom (James, 1989).

## Key Takeaways
- In 1789, Saint-Domingue was France's most profitable colony, producing about 40 percent of Europe's sugar and about 60 percent of its coffee on the forced labor of roughly 500,000 enslaved Africans (James, 1989; Smith, 2005).
- Enslaved leaders planned the uprising at the Bois Caiman ceremony in August 1791, and the revolt began on August 22, 1791 (James, 1989).
- France abolished slavery in the colony in 1793, and Haiti declared independence on January 1, 1804 (James, 1989).
- It was the only successful large-scale slave revolution in the Western Hemisphere and produced the first Black republic, with enslaved people acting as strategic political agents (James, 1989).

## Sources
- James, C. L. R. (1989). The Black Jacobins: Toussaint L'Ouverture and the San Domingo Revolution (2nd ed.). Vintage Books.
- Smith, F. H. (2005). Caribbean rum: A social and economic history. University Press of Florida.
- Beckford, G. L., & Campbell, M. (2013). Persistent poverty: Underdevelopment in plantation economies of the Third World. University of the West Indies Press.

title: The Debt of Freedom
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain the 1825 French indemnity and how it ties colonial slavery directly to Haiti's modern poverty.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we covered the Haitian Revolution. Two quick questions.
One: what made the Haitian Revolution historically unique? [Beat] It was the only successful large-scale slave revolution in the Western Hemisphere, and it created the first Black republic (James, 1989).
Two: when did Haiti declare independence? [Beat] January 1, 1804 (James, 1989).

[HOOK] Imagine you free yourself from bondage. You fight a war, you win, you build a country. And then the people who used to hold you captive send a bill. Not to you for damages they caused, but to you, for the cost of yourself. That is close to what happened to Haiti, and the country is still living with the result.

[TEACH] Start with the standoff. After 1804, Haiti was free, but France refused to recognize it as a nation. France set a price for that recognition. Haiti would have to pay French slaveholders 150 million gold francs to compensate them for their "lost property" (Getachew, 2019). Read that carefully. The "property" was the people who had freed themselves. France was demanding that formerly enslaved people pay their former enslavers for the freedom they had already won.
Now the pressure. Haiti did not simply agree to this. France backed the demand with the threat of renewed military force, so in 1825 Haiti agreed under that threat (Getachew, 2019). Here is the part that turns a hard demand into a long trap. Haiti did not have 150 million gold francs lying around. So Haiti borrowed the money from French banks to pay French slaveholders (Getachew, 2019). One set of French institutions collected the payments while another collected the interest on the loans. This is why historians call it a "double debt." Haiti paid for the same freedom twice, once to the slaveholders and once to the banks.
Follow the timeline forward. The debt was renegotiated more than once, and Haiti kept paying versions of it through 1947 (Getachew, 2019). That is over a century of a young, poor nation sending money out to its former colonizer instead of building roads, schools, and industry at home. Economists who have added up the payments and the interest estimate the total cost in modern terms at roughly 21 billion dollars (Getachew, 2019). This is the straight line we need to see. Saint-Domingue's wealth came from sugar and rum made by enslaved people (Smith, 2005). When those people freed themselves, the same colonial system used a legal and financial mechanism, not an army, to keep extracting from them. Colonialism did not end in 1804. It changed its tools.

[PICTURE] Picture two ledgers side by side. In the first, before 1804, the value flows one way, out of Haiti to France, as sugar and rum. In the second, after 1825, the value still flows the same direction, but now it is gold francs, year after year, decade after decade, until 1947. Same direction, different label.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: in 1825, under threat of French force, Haiti agreed to pay French slaveholders 150 million gold francs to compensate them for the people who had freed themselves, borrowed from French banks to do it, and paid versions of that debt through 1947, at a total modern cost estimated near 21 billion dollars (Getachew, 2019). That "double debt" is a direct line from colonial slavery to Haiti's long economic crippling.

[CHECK] Quick check. What did the 1825 indemnity force Haiti to pay for, and why does it matter? [Beat] It forced Haiti to pay French slaveholders for the "loss" of the enslaved people who had freed themselves, financed by loans from French banks, which drained the nation for over a century and connects colonial slavery directly to Haiti's modern poverty (Getachew, 2019).

## Key Takeaways
- After independence, France refused to recognize Haiti unless it paid French slaveholders 150 million gold francs to compensate them for their "lost property," the people who had freed themselves (Getachew, 2019).
- Threatened with renewed French force, Haiti agreed in 1825 and borrowed from French banks to pay French slaveholders, creating a "double debt" (Getachew, 2019).
- Haiti paid versions of the debt through 1947, at a total modern cost economists estimate near 21 billion dollars (Getachew, 2019).
- The indemnity is a direct line from colonial slavery, which produced Saint-Domingue's sugar and rum wealth, to Haiti's long economic crippling (Getachew, 2019; Smith, 2005).

## Sources
- Getachew, A. (2019). Worldmaking after empire: The rise and fall of self-determination. Princeton University Press.
- Smith, F. H. (2005). Caribbean rum: A social and economic history. University Press of Florida.
- James, C. L. R. (1989). The Black Jacobins: Toussaint L'Ouverture and the San Domingo Revolution (2nd ed.). Vintage Books.

title: The Maroons
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how Maroon communities built freedom and self-government inside the plantation world.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we covered the debt of freedom. Two quick questions.
One: what did the 1825 indemnity force Haiti to pay for? [Beat] It forced Haiti to pay French slaveholders for the "loss" of the enslaved people who had freed themselves (Getachew, 2019).
Two: roughly what was its cost in modern dollars? [Beat] About 21 billion dollars over the full life of the debt (Getachew, 2019).

[HOOK] The Haitian Revolution was the largest act of self-liberation in the Atlantic world, but it was not the only one. Across the Americas, smaller groups of enslaved people did something just as bold on a different scale. They walked off the plantation, went into the mountains and forests, and built their own free societies. Some of those societies are still here today.

[TEACH] Start with the name and the idea. These people are called Maroons, self-liberated enslaved people who escaped and built independent communities of their own (Price, 1996). They did not wait for an empire to grant freedom. They took it, then built something to defend and sustain it. Look at three examples, because the differences tell the story.
First, Accompong Town in Jamaica's Cockpit Country. The Accompong Maroons fought the British long enough to force a treaty in 1739 that recognized their autonomy, and their descendants maintain that autonomy and their traditions today (Price, 1996). Second, Palmares in Brazil. Palmares was the largest Maroon state, a community of roughly 20,000 people that lasted from 1605 to 1694 before Portuguese forces finally destroyed it (Price, 1996). Sit with that. Nearly a century of independence in the middle of a slave empire. Third, the Saramaka of Suriname, who built communities in the rainforest interior and whose land rights were recognized by the Inter-American Court of Human Rights in 2007 (Price, 1996). One long fight that ran from escape all the way into a modern international court.
Now see what they actually built, because "ran away" is not the right picture. Maroon communities built autonomous governance, their own systems of law and leadership (Price, 1996). They built independent farming economies that did not depend on the plantation (Price, 1996). They preserved African cultural traditions in the diaspora, keeping languages, religions, and practices alive (Price, 1996). And they developed real military skill, the knowledge needed to resist recapture for years or generations (Price, 1996). This was nation-building, not just escape.
Hold the hard part too, because honest history includes it. Some Maroon communities won their autonomy through treaties that required them to return people who escaped after the treaty was signed (Price, 1996). The British treaty with the Jamaican Maroons in 1739 is the clearest case. Freedom for one community was sometimes purchased at the cost of others still in bondage. That is a genuine moral complexity, and historians still work through it (Price, 1996). It does not erase what the Maroons built. It shows how brutal the system was, that it could force even free people into terrible bargains.

[PICTURE] Picture the edge of a plantation at night. The cane fields end, and the land rises into mountains the planters cannot easily follow. Somewhere up there is a town with its own elected leaders, its own farms, its own ceremonies in a language carried across the ocean. It has been there for decades. It plans to stay.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: Maroons were self-liberated enslaved people who built independent communities such as Accompong Town in Jamaica (treaty in 1739), Palmares in Brazil (about 20,000 people, 1605 to 1694), and the Saramaka of Suriname (recognized by the Inter-American Court of Human Rights in 2007), with their own governance, farming, culture, and military skill, though some won autonomy through treaties that required returning newly escaped people (Price, 1996). Resistance built whole societies, not just escapes.

[CHECK] Quick check. Describe one Maroon community and what it shows about resistance within the plantation system. [Beat] Palmares in Brazil was the largest Maroon state, about 20,000 people, lasting from 1605 to 1694, which shows that self-liberated people could build and defend an independent society for nearly a century inside a slave empire (Price, 1996).

## Key Takeaways
- Maroons were self-liberated enslaved people who escaped and built their own independent communities across the Americas (Price, 1996).
- Examples span centuries and regions: Accompong Town in Jamaica (treaty with the British in 1739), Palmares in Brazil (about 20,000 people, 1605 to 1694), and the Saramaka of Suriname (recognized by the Inter-American Court of Human Rights in 2007) (Price, 1996).
- Maroon communities built autonomous governance, independent farming economies, preserved African cultural traditions, and the military skill to resist recapture (Price, 1996).
- Some communities won autonomy through treaties that required returning newly escaped people, a real moral complexity historians still study (Price, 1996).

## Sources
- Price, R. (1996). Maroon societies: Rebel slave communities in the Americas (3rd ed.). Johns Hopkins University Press.
- Getachew, A. (2019). Worldmaking after empire: The rise and fall of self-determination. Princeton University Press.

title: Waste-Product Economics
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain why rum was so profitable for plantation owners and how that connects to a famous economic thesis.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we talked about the Maroons. Two quick questions.
One: what was a Maroon community? [Beat] A community of self-liberated people who escaped slavery and built free settlements, often in remote mountains or forests (Price, 1996).
Two: name one Maroon community. [Beat] Accompong in Jamaica, or Palmares in Brazil (Price, 1996).

[HOOK] Here is an uncomfortable truth about money. By the cold logic of profit, rum was one of the smartest products a plantation owner could make. To understand why, you have to look at what each part of it cost. And what you find is that almost every cost was, on paper, near zero.

[TEACH] Start with the raw material. When a sugar mill boiled cane juice to make crystallized white sugar, it left behind molasses, thick and dark. To a 17th-century planter, molasses was waste. It cost nothing because nobody was paying for it (Smith, 2005). That is the first near-zero cost.
Next, the labor. Turning molasses into rum takes work. You add water, let it ferment, then distill it. On a Caribbean plantation, enslaved workers did that distilling, alongside the sugar work, so the planter paid nothing for the labor (Smith, 2005). That is the second near-zero cost.
Then the equipment. A still and the distilling gear cost something, but it was a modest one-time purchase, not an ongoing bill. Buy it once, use it for years.
Now the other side of the ledger. Rum sold for real money in real markets, in the islands, in New England, in Europe. So you had a product made from free waste, by unpaid labor, on a one-time piece of equipment, that you could sell for cash. That is high margin on near-zero cost (Smith, 2005). By the numbers a planter chose to count, it was almost pure profit.
Now hold the bigger idea, called double extraction. The planter already made money selling the sugar. Then the planter made money again selling the rum, the so-called waste from that same sugar. Same land. Same enslaved labor force. Two streams of profit (Williams, 1994). That double catch is what made Caribbean plantation economics among the most profitable enterprises in 17th and 18th century capitalism.
And that profit did not just sit in the islands. The historian Eric Williams, in his 1944 book Capitalism and Slavery, argued that the profits from the slave trade and from plantation production were a major source of the capital that funded the British Industrial Revolution (Williams, 1994). In other words, money squeezed out of enslaved people and waste molasses helped build the factories of modern Britain. That argument set off decades of debate among historians, and it is now broadly, though not universally, accepted.
Here is the point to sit with. This whole system was judged profitable by the people running it, because they only counted the metrics they chose to count: cash in, cash out. They did not count the human beings as anything but property. Choose a different metric, one that counts human welfare, and the same system that looks brilliantly profitable looks like one of the great crimes of its age. The math does not change. What you decide to measure does.

[PICTURE] Picture the ledger on a planter's desk. One column reads molasses, cost zero. The next reads labor, cost zero. The next reads still, paid for years ago. The last column reads rum sold, full of numbers in black ink. Every line that should hold a human cost is simply blank, because the bookkeeping was built to leave it out.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: rum was high margin on near-zero cost, free waste molasses, unpaid enslaved labor, a one-time still, sold for real money, and through double extraction planters profited twice from the same land and people, which is why Eric Williams argued these profits helped fund the British Industrial Revolution (Williams, 1994; Smith, 2005). A system its operators called profitable looks very different when you count human welfare.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why was rum so profitable for plantation owners? [Beat] Because its main costs were near zero. Molasses was free waste, enslaved workers did the distilling for no pay, and the still was a modest one-time cost, so rum sold for real money at very high margin, and planters captured value from both sugar and rum off the same labor (Smith, 2005; Williams, 1994).

## Key Takeaways
- Rum was high margin on near-zero cost: free waste molasses, unpaid enslaved labor, and a one-time still (Smith, 2005).
- Double extraction meant planters profited twice, from sugar and from rum, off the same land and same enslaved labor force (Williams, 1994).
- This made Caribbean plantation economics among the most profitable enterprises in 17th and 18th century capitalism.
- Eric Williams argued these profits were a major source of capital for the British Industrial Revolution, a thesis now broadly accepted (Williams, 1994).
- A system its operators called profitable looks very different once human welfare is counted.

## Sources
- Williams, E. (1994). Capitalism and slavery. University of North Carolina Press.
- Smith, F. H. (2005). Caribbean rum: A social and economic history. University Press of Florida.

title: Who Gets Paid, and Martinique's Answer
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how value flows in the modern rum trade and how Martinique asserts economic sovereignty.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we talked about waste-product economics. Two quick questions.
One: why was rum so profitable for planters? [Beat] Its costs were near zero. Free waste molasses, unpaid enslaved labor, and a one-time still, sold for real money at high margin (Smith, 2005).
Two: what did Eric Williams argue? [Beat] That profits from the slave trade and plantation production were a major source of capital for the British Industrial Revolution (Williams, 1994).

[HOOK] That was the old extraction. Now jump to today. You buy a nice bottle of premium Caribbean rum off a shelf for 40 dollars. The question for this lesson is simple but revealing. Of that 40 dollars, who actually gets paid? Follow the money, and the pattern from history did not really go away. But you will also find one island that decided to fight it.

[TEACH] Walk the chain on a 40 dollar bottle (Smith, 2005). The sugarcane farmer who grew the cane captures roughly 50 cents to 1 dollar. The distillery that turned it into rum captures a few dollars. So the people on the island who actually made the thing get a thin slice off the top.
Now look at where the rest goes. The biggest shares of that 40 dollars flow to brand marketing, to distribution, and to retail, and most of that happens in the wealthy country where the bottle is sold, not on the island that produced it (Smith, 2005).
Put a number on how lopsided that is. Caribbean rum producers, the distilleries, capture about 12 to 25 percent of the retail price. That is actually better than what we saw in Season 1 with coffee, where Ethiopian farmers captured only about 6 to 10 percent of retail (Smith, 2005). Better, yes. But still a structure that pulls value toward the rich consuming country. The commodity changed from coffee to rum. The extractive shape stayed.
Now here is the island that answered back. Martinique. In 1996, Martinique won something no other rum on Earth has, an AOC, Appellation d'Origine Controlee, the same protected-origin label France uses for its wines (Martinique AOC, 1996). To call your rum Martinique rhum agricole, it must be made on the island, from local fresh-pressed sugarcane juice, by specified methods. Nobody else can borrow the name.
Watch what that does to the money. AOC rhum agricole from Martinique sells for about 40 to 80 dollars a bottle, while ordinary molasses-based Caribbean rum sells for about 15 to 30 (Martinique AOC, 1996; IWSR Drinks Market Analysis, 2024). The protected name creates a price premium, blocks imitators, and concentrates that extra value on the island instead of letting it drain away.
And here is the part worth admiring. The AOC system is French. France built it to protect French wine regions. Martinique, a former French colony, took the colonizer's own legal tool and used it to protect a Caribbean product and claim economic control over it (Martinique AOC, 1996). That is economic sovereignty, real power over your own resources, won not by fighting the existing institutions but by working inside them.

[PICTURE] Picture two bottles on a shelf. One is generic molasses rum at 20 dollars, and most of those dollars end up with a distributor and a retailer far from the island. Beside it is a Martinique rhum agricole at 60 dollars, wearing a French legal seal that says, made here, by us, and you cannot copy the name. More of those sixty dollars stay home.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: in the modern rum trade most retail value accrues in the wealthy consuming country, with Caribbean producers capturing only about 12 to 25 percent of retail, but Martinique's 1996 rhum agricole AOC uses the colonizer's own French legal system to command premium prices and keep value on the island, a form of economic sovereignty (Smith, 2005; Martinique AOC, 1996; IWSR Drinks Market Analysis, 2024). The producing island can claim power inside the existing system.

[CHECK] Quick check. How does Martinique's AOC function as economic sovereignty? [Beat] By using the French protected-origin system to legally reserve the name Martinique rhum agricole for rum made on the island, which creates a price premium of about 40 to 80 dollars a bottle and concentrates value on the island, a former colony using the colonizer's own legal tools to control its own resource (Martinique AOC, 1996).

## Key Takeaways
- On a 40 dollar bottle, the cane farmer captures roughly 50 cents to 1 dollar and the distillery a few dollars, while marketing, distribution, and retail take the largest shares (Smith, 2005).
- Most retail value accrues in the wealthy consuming country, not the producing island.
- Caribbean rum producers capture about 12 to 25 percent of retail, better than Ethiopian coffee farmers' roughly 6 to 10 percent, but still extractive (Smith, 2005).
- Martinique's 1996 rhum agricole AOC commands about 40 to 80 dollars a bottle versus 15 to 30 for molasses rum, a former colony using the colonizer's own legal system to keep value on the island (Martinique AOC, 1996; IWSR Drinks Market Analysis, 2024).

## Sources
- Smith, F. H. (2005). Caribbean rum: A social and economic history. University Press of Florida.
- Martinique AOC. (1996). Appellation d'Origine Controlee: Martinique Rhum Agricole. Institut National de l'Origine et de la Qualite (INAO).
- IWSR Drinks Market Analysis. (2024). Global rum market report 2024. IWSR.

title: The Black Jacobins
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze how C.L.R. James used historical writing to make an argument about enslaved people as political agents.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we asked who gets paid, and looked at Martinique's answer. Two quick questions.
One: how does Martinique's AOC assert economic sovereignty? [Beat] By legally protecting the name and method of its rhum agricole, so the island controls and profits from its own product instead of competitors copying it (Smith, 2005).
Two: roughly what share of a rum bottle's price reaches the cane farmer? [Beat] Only a small slice, often a few cents on the dollar, while most of the price goes to brand owners, distributors, and retailers.

[HOOK] Here is a quiet trick a writer can play. Before you read a single page of a history book, the title has already made an argument. The book on this lesson is called The Black Jacobins. Three words, and a claim is already on the table.

[TEACH] The Black Jacobins was published in 1938 and revised in 1963 by C.L.R. James, a Trinidadian writer (James, 1989). It is a history of the Haitian Revolution, the uprising in which enslaved people in the French colony of Saint-Domingue overthrew their enslavers and founded a free nation. James built the book on careful archival research, working through French records, English correspondence, and Haitian sources (James, 1989). It is real history, documented in detail.
But James was doing more than recording events. His central argument was that enslaved people were not passive recipients of history. He showed them as active political agents who made strategy, built coalitions, and created new political forms (James, 1989). That word, agents, matters. An agent is someone who acts and chooses, not someone things merely happen to. Much earlier writing had treated enslaved people as a background to other people's decisions. James put their decisions at the center.
Now hear the title again. The Jacobins were the most radical faction of the French Revolution, the people who pushed hardest for liberty, equality, and fraternity. By calling the Haitian revolutionaries the Black Jacobins, James makes a claim before chapter one. He says these enslaved people understood France's own stated ideals better than France did, and they held France to them (James, 1989). The title is an argument dressed as a name.
This is the big idea: historical writing is argument. James made choices. He chose which sources to lean on. He chose to frame Toussaint L'Ouverture, the revolution's central leader, as a strategic genius rather than a victim. He chose to compare Haiti to the French Revolution rather than treat it as a strange exception (James, 1989). Each choice advances a claim about who has political capacity and who makes history. A historian is never just listing facts. The selection and the framing are the argument.

[PICTURE] Picture two ways to write the same scene. In one, enslaved people appear only when someone frees them, waiting in the margins. In the other, James's version, they are in the council, drawing up plans, weighing alliances, deciding when to strike. Same events, two different stories, and the second one insists these are people who think and choose. The framing is the message.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: C.L.R. James's The Black Jacobins documents the Haitian Revolution through archival research while arguing that enslaved people were active political agents, and even the title, naming them after the radical French Jacobins, makes that argument by holding France to its own ideals of liberty, equality, and fraternity (James, 1989). Historical writing is argument, not just record.

[CHECK] Quick check. How does the title The Black Jacobins make an argument before you read the book? [Beat] It names the enslaved revolutionaries after the most radical French revolutionaries, claiming they grasped and acted on France's ideals of liberty, equality, and fraternity, and holding France to its own stated values (James, 1989).

## Key Takeaways
- The Black Jacobins (1938, revised 1963) by Trinidadian writer C.L.R. James is a researched history of the Haitian Revolution (James, 1989).
- James argued that enslaved people were active political agents who made strategy and built coalitions, not passive victims (James, 1989).
- The title itself argues, naming the revolutionaries after the radical French Jacobins to hold France to its ideals (James, 1989).
- Historical writing is argument, since the choice of sources, framing, and comparison all advance a claim.

## Sources
- James, C. L. R. (1989). The Black Jacobins: Toussaint L'Ouverture and the San Domingo Revolution (2nd ed.). Vintage Books.

title: Tiki and the Tropical Paradise
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze how tiki culture used narrative to erase the real history of Caribbean rum.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we read The Black Jacobins. Two quick questions.
One: what did C.L.R. James argue about enslaved people? [Beat] That they were active political agents who made strategy and built coalitions, not passive victims of history (James, 1989).
Two: how does the book's title make an argument? [Beat] By naming the revolutionaries after the radical French Jacobins, it claims they understood and acted on France's ideals and holds France to them (James, 1989).

[HOOK] If James used narrative to reveal a hidden history, here is a narrative built to do the opposite, to bury one. Walk into a mid-century tiki bar, carved wooden faces on the wall, a fruity rum drink in your hand, and a story whispering in your ear that you have escaped to paradise. The question to ask is, escape from what, and whose paradise.

[TEACH] Tiki was a mid-20th century American phenomenon. It combined three things: Caribbean rum cocktails, a fantasy look that borrowed Polynesian visual imagery like carved figures and thatching, and a tropical paradise story (Smith, 2005). All three were assembled far from the places they referenced, for American customers.
Look at it as appropriation in layers. The rum came from the Caribbean, produced through a history of enslaved labor. The carved figures came from Polynesian cultures, used as bar decoration with no consent and no cultural context. And the tropical paradise story came from the colonial imagination, a fantasy of simple, carefree islands (Smith, 2005). Notice the geography does not even match. Caribbean rum, Pacific carvings, all blurred into one exotic backdrop. The specifics of real cultures got flattened into a mood.
Now do a close reading of the phrase tropical paradise, because the words are doing quiet work. Tropical marks a warm, remote place. Paradise implies innocent simplicity, a kind of Eden. Put them together and they suggest a place outside history, with none of the complications of real life (Smith, 2005). And that is exactly the problem. To call these islands a paradise outside history, you have to erase the actual history, the slavery, the colonialism, and the real complexity of Caribbean and Pacific cultures. The phrase is comfortable precisely because it deletes what is uncomfortable.
Compare the two narratives we have now met. James chose framing that revealed agency and history. Tiki chose framing that erased them. Both are narrative constructions, both are choices, and the choices point in opposite directions. That is the lesson: a narrative is defined as much by what it leaves out as by what it puts in.
This leaves a genuine open question, and it is worth real debate, not a quick answer. What does thoughtful, critical consumption look like today? Can you enjoy a rum cocktail while knowing this history? Who gets to decide whether a use is appreciation or appropriation? There is no tidy resolution here. The point is to keep the real history in view rather than let a pleasant phrase erase it.

[PICTURE] Picture the carved tiki mug on the bar. To the customer in 1955 it reads as fun and far away. Read it slowly instead. A spirit made through enslaved labor, poured into a vessel that copies a sacred Pacific figure, sold under a story that says none of that ever happened. One object, several erasures, stacked under a single word, paradise.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: tiki was a mid-20th century American narrative that combined Caribbean rum, borrowed Polynesian imagery, and a tropical paradise story, and that phrase erased slavery, colonialism, and the real complexity of Caribbean and Pacific cultures by suggesting a place outside history (Smith, 2005). A narrative erases as much by what it omits as by what it states.

[CHECK] Quick check. What does the phrase tropical paradise erase, and how? [Beat] By marking the islands as warm, remote, and innocently simple, a place outside history, it erases the actual slavery, colonialism, and real cultural complexity of the Caribbean and the Pacific (Smith, 2005).

## Key Takeaways
- Tiki was a mid-20th century American phenomenon combining Caribbean rum, borrowed Polynesian imagery, and a tropical paradise story (Smith, 2005).
- It was appropriation in layers: rum from the Caribbean, carvings from Polynesian cultures used without context, and a story from the colonial imagination (Smith, 2005).
- The phrase tropical paradise suggests a place outside history and so erases slavery, colonialism, and real cultural complexity (Smith, 2005).
- What critical, thoughtful consumption looks like today is a genuine open question worth debate.

## Sources
- Smith, F. H. (2005). Caribbean rum: A social and economic history. University Press of Florida.

title: Key Terms: Rum
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can define the core vocabulary from Episode 11 across all four lenses.
Subject: Vocabulary | Run time: about 5 minutes

Your word bank for the Rum episode. Read it quick, out loud if you can. The full glossary lives in the course Resources.

## Geography

**Saccharum officinarum** (SAK-uh-rum oh-fiss-ih-NAR-um). Sugarcane, the tropical grass whose juice and molasses make rum (Galloway, 1989).

**Rhum agricole** (ROOM ah-gree-KOHL). Rum made from fresh-pressed cane juice rather than molasses, showing true terroir (Smith, 2005).

**Dunder** (DUN-der). Leftover liquid from a previous distillation, added in Jamaica to deepen the funk (Smith, 2005).

**Triangular Trade.** The three-leg Atlantic circuit of goods to Africa, enslaved people to the Americas, and sugar and rum back (Slave Voyages Database, 2024).

**Middle Passage.** The forced Atlantic crossing carrying enslaved Africans, the deadly second leg of the trade (Slave Voyages Database, 2024).

## Social Studies

**Saint-Domingue** (san-doh-MANG). France's most profitable colony, built on enslaved labor, that became independent Haiti in 1804 (James, 1989).

**Indemnity** (in-DEM-nih-tee). The 150 million gold francs France forced Haiti to pay slaveholders in 1825 for the people who had freed themselves (Getachew, 2019).

**Maroon** (muh-ROON). A self-liberated enslaved person, or a member of an independent community of them (Price, 1996).

## Economics

**Molasses** (muh-LASS-iz). The thick byproduct of refining sugar, rum's original raw material (Williams, 1994).

**Double extraction.** Capturing the value of both sugar and rum from the same land and same enslaved labor (Williams, 1994).

**Economic sovereignty.** Control over one's own economic resources, as Martinique asserts through its rum AOC (Smith, 2005).

**AOC.** A protected geographic designation, like Martinique rhum agricole, that concentrates value locally (Martinique AOC, 1996).

## English Language Arts

**Agency** (AY-jen-see). The capacity to act independently and make choices, as enslaved people did in Haiti (James, 1989).

**Counter-narrative.** A story told to challenge a dominant narrative, like Haitian history against the tiki fantasy (James, 1989).

**Appropriation** (uh-proh-pree-AY-shun). Taking cultural elements from a marginalized group without permission or understanding (Smith, 2005).

**Erasure** (ee-RAY-zher). Omitting or overwriting a group's experiences, as tiki erased Caribbean history (Smith, 2005).

## Sources
- Galloway, J. H. (1989). The sugar cane industry: An historical geography from its origins to 1914. Cambridge University Press.
- Getachew, A. (2019). Worldmaking after empire. Princeton University Press.
- James, C. L. R. (1989). The Black Jacobins (2nd ed.). Vintage Books.
- Martinique AOC. (1996). Appellation d'Origine Controlee: Martinique Rhum Agricole. INAO.
- Price, R. (1996). Maroon societies: Rebel slave communities in the Americas (3rd ed.). Johns Hopkins University Press.
- Slave Voyages Database. (2024). Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database. Emory University.
- Smith, F. H. (2005). Caribbean rum: A social and economic history. University Press of Florida.
- Williams, E. (1994). Capitalism and slavery. University of North Carolina Press.

title: Cumulative Review: Rum
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can recall the top facts from all four lenses and see how they connect.
Subject: Review | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Let's pull the Rum episode together, one quick fact per lens, then the thread that ties them.

[TEACH] Start with geography. The Caribbean's tropical climate and volcanic soil made it close to perfect for sugarcane, and colonial powers used that fitness as an argument to justify enslaving millions (Galloway, 1989; Smith, 2005). Each island makes a different rum, from funky Jamaica to light Cuba to Martinique's cane-juice agricole. The trade itself drew a triangle on the map, with about 12.5 million Africans carried across the Middle Passage (Slave Voyages Database, 2024). And the islands made richest by sugar are now among the most economically challenged and climate-vulnerable in the hemisphere (Beckford & Campbell, 2013).

Now social studies. In Saint-Domingue, enslaved people won the only successful large-scale slave revolution and founded Haiti, the first Black republic, in 1804 (James, 1989). France punished that freedom with an 1825 indemnity worth roughly 21 billion modern dollars, paid through 1947 (Getachew, 2019). And across the Americas, Maroons freed themselves and built lasting autonomous communities like Accompong (Price, 1996).

Then economics. Rum was pure profit for planters because molasses was free waste and the labor was enslaved, a double extraction (Williams, 1994). Most of a rum bottle's value still flows to the consuming country, but Martinique uses a French AOC to assert economic sovereignty over its own product (Smith, 2005; Martinique AOC, 1996).

Last, language. C.L.R. James wrote The Black Jacobins to show enslaved people as political agents, not passive victims, with the title itself making the argument (James, 1989). And tiki culture did the opposite, using a tropical-paradise fantasy to erase the real history of slavery and colonialism (Smith, 2005).

[REMEMBER] Here is the thread. Rum is the spirit of sugar, and sugar was built on the forced labor of enslaved people. Geography shows how the map was weaponized, history shows both the cruelty and the resistance, economics shows who was robbed and who fought back, and language shows the difference between a story that tells the truth and one that hides it. Sweet and poison, held together.

[CHECK] One last check before the quiz. Why is this episode called Sweet Poison? [Beat] Rum is the sweet spirit of sugar, but its history is bound to the cruelty of the Atlantic slave trade, so an honest telling holds both at once (Smith, 2005).

## Key Takeaways
- Geography: the Caribbean's fitness for cane was weaponized to justify slavery, and the trade ran on the Middle Passage (Slave Voyages Database, 2024).
- Social studies: Haiti won the only successful slave revolution but was crippled by France's 1825 indemnity, while Maroons built lasting freedom (James, 1989; Getachew, 2019; Price, 1996).
- Economics: rum was profitable through double extraction, and Martinique now uses an AOC for economic sovereignty (Williams, 1994; Smith, 2005).
- Language: C.L.R. James restored enslaved people's agency, while tiki culture erased the real history (James, 1989).

## Sources
- Beckford, G. L., & Campbell, M. (2013). Persistent poverty. University of the West Indies Press.
- Galloway, J. H. (1989). The sugar cane industry. Cambridge University Press.
- Getachew, A. (2019). Worldmaking after empire. Princeton University Press.
- James, C. L. R. (1989). The Black Jacobins (2nd ed.). Vintage Books.
- Martinique AOC. (1996). Appellation d'Origine Controlee: Martinique Rhum Agricole. INAO.
- Price, R. (1996). Maroon societies. Johns Hopkins University Press.
- Slave Voyages Database. (2024). Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database. Emory University.
- Smith, F. H. (2005). Caribbean rum: A social and economic history. University Press of Florida.
- Williams, E. (1994). Capitalism and slavery. University of North Carolina Press.

James, C. L. R. (1989). The Black Jacobins: Toussaint L'Ouverture and the San Domingo Revolution (2nd ed.). Vintage Books.

Martinique AOC. (1996). Appellation d'Origine Controlee: Martinique Rhum Agricole. Institut National de l'Origine et de la Qualite (INAO).

McCusker, J. J. (1970). Rum and the American Revolution: The rum trade and the balance of payments of the thirteen continental colonies. Garland Publishing.

Mintz, S. W. (1985). Sweetness and power: The place of sugar in modern history. Penguin Books.

Pack, J. (1982). Nelson's blood: The story of naval rum. Naval Institute Press.

Price, R. (1996). Maroon societies: Rebel slave communities in the Americas (3rd ed.). Johns Hopkins University Press.

Slave Voyages Database. (2024). Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database. Emory University. https://www.slavevoyages.org/

Smith, F. H. (2005). Caribbean rum: A social and economic history. University Press of Florida.

Thomas, H. (1997). The slave trade: The story of the Atlantic slave trade, 1440-1870. Simon & Schuster.
