title: Knowledge Check: Rum
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 11: geography, social studies, economics, and language.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

This is a heavy episode, and that is the point. When you finish, take the Project assignment, where the hardest questions, how geography was weaponized and who history remembers, get the long-form treatment they deserve.
quiz_content: {"passingScore":80,"attemptsAllowed":-1,"questionsPerAttempt":10,"shuffleOptions":true,"questions":[{"id":"q1","questionText":"What waste product is rum originally made from?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q1a","text":"Molasses, the byproduct of refining sugar"},{"id":"q1b","text":"Grape skins"},{"id":"q1c","text":"Barley husks"},{"id":"q1d","text":"Corn cobs"}],"correctOptionId":"q1a","explanation":"Rum began as a way to make money from molasses, the sticky waste left over from refining sugar, produced by the forced labor of enslaved Africans.","citation":"Lesson: Sweet Poison (intro)"},{"id":"q2","questionText":"How was the Caribbean's suitability for sugarcane used by colonial powers?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q2a","text":"As an argument to justify enslaving Africans to do the brutal work"},{"id":"q2b","text":"To protect the local population"},{"id":"q2c","text":"To pay fair wages"},{"id":"q2d","text":"It had no effect on labor"}],"correctOptionId":"q2a","explanation":"Colonial planners used the land's fitness for cane as economic logic to justify forced labor. The geography did not cause slavery; people chose it and used the map to excuse it.","citation":"Lesson: Why the Caribbean"},{"id":"q3","questionText":"What does sugarcane (Saccharum officinarum) need to grow?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q3a","text":"Year-round heat, heavy rain, rich soil, strong sun, and no frost"},{"id":"q3b","text":"Cold winters and dry summers"},{"id":"q3c","text":"Desert conditions"},{"id":"q3d","text":"Mountain snow"}],"correctOptionId":"q3a","explanation":"Sugarcane needs roughly 70 to 95 degree heat all year, 50 to 80 inches of rain, rich well-drained soil, abundant sun, and no frost, which the tropical Caribbean matched.","citation":"Lesson: Why the Caribbean"},{"id":"q4","questionText":"What is the difference between molasses rum and rhum agricole?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q4a","text":"Molasses rum is made from the sugar byproduct; rhum agricole is made from fresh cane juice and shows true terroir"},{"id":"q4b","text":"They are the same thing"},{"id":"q4c","text":"Rhum agricole is made from grapes"},{"id":"q4d","text":"Molasses rum uses no sugar"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Molasses rum reflects distillery tradition, while rhum agricole, made from fresh-pressed cane juice, captures the season and soil of a specific island.","citation":"Lesson: Different Islands, Different Rums"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"Why does Jamaican rum taste so different from Cuban rum?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"Jamaica uses heavy pot stills for funky, high-ester rum; Cuba uses column stills for light, clean rum"},{"id":"q5b","text":"They use different fruits"},{"id":"q5c","text":"Cuba does not make rum"},{"id":"q5d","text":"There is no difference"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"Jamaica's pot-still, high-ester rum is funky and built for aging, while Cuba's column-still rum is light and made for cocktails, so each island leaves its own fingerprint.","citation":"Lesson: Different Islands, Different Rums"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"What were the three legs of the triangular trade?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"Goods to West Africa, enslaved people across the Middle Passage, and sugar and rum back to consumers"},{"id":"q6b","text":"Three trips all within the Caribbean"},{"id":"q6c","text":"Europe to Asia to Africa"},{"id":"q6d","text":"There was only one leg"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"Ships carried rum and goods to Africa, enslaved Africans across the Middle Passage to the Caribbean, and sugar, molasses, and rum back to Europe and New England.","citation":"Lesson: The Triangular Trade"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"Roughly how many Africans were trafficked across the Atlantic between 1500 and 1900?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"About 12.5 million, with roughly 1.8 million dying on the crossing"},{"id":"q7b","text":"About 1,000"},{"id":"q7c","text":"About 50,000"},{"id":"q7d","text":"None"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"According to the Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database, about 12.5 million Africans were trafficked, and roughly 1.8 million died during the Middle Passage.","citation":"Lesson: The Triangular Trade"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"What were rum's two roles in the triangular trade?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"A product of enslaved labor and a currency used to buy more enslaved people"},{"id":"q8b","text":"A medicine and a fuel"},{"id":"q8c","text":"A food and a cleaner"},{"id":"q8d","text":"It had no role"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"Rum was distilled by enslaved labor in the Caribbean and New England, and it was carried to Africa as a trade good to buy more enslaved people, sitting on two legs of the triangle.","citation":"Lesson: The Triangular Trade"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"Why are many Caribbean nations economically challenged today?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"Colonial plantation economies extracted wealth without reinvesting locally, building poverty into the system"},{"id":"q9b","text":"They never grew anything valuable"},{"id":"q9c","text":"They refused all trade"},{"id":"q9d","text":"It is pure bad luck"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"Plantation economies shipped wealth out to faraway owners without building local infrastructure, schools, or industry, so the poverty was structured into the system from the start.","citation":"Lesson: The Caribbean Today"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"What is the geographic irony of the Caribbean today?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"The same tropical geography that made it valuable for sugar now makes it highly vulnerable to climate change"},{"id":"q10b","text":"It is now too cold for crops"},{"id":"q10c","text":"It has no coastline"},{"id":"q10d","text":"It is immune to hurricanes"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"The tropical, low-lying, ocean-surrounded position that drew the colonizers now exposes the Caribbean to stronger hurricanes, sea level rise, and shifting rainfall.","citation":"Lesson: The Caribbean Today"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"What made Saint-Domingue (Haiti) so valuable to France in 1789?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"It produced about 40 percent of Europe's sugar and 60 percent of its coffee on enslaved labor"},{"id":"q11b","text":"It had gold mines"},{"id":"q11c","text":"It was a military base only"},{"id":"q11d","text":"It produced nothing"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"Saint-Domingue was France's most profitable colony, producing roughly 40 percent of Europe's sugar and 60 percent of its coffee, on the forced labor of about 500,000 enslaved people.","citation":"Lesson: The Haitian Revolution"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"Why is the Haitian Revolution historically unique?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"It was the only successful large-scale slave revolution, creating the first Black republic in 1804"},{"id":"q12b","text":"It failed completely"},{"id":"q12c","text":"It was led by France"},{"id":"q12d","text":"It never happened"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"Beginning in 1791 and declaring independence on January 1, 1804, it was the only successful large-scale slave revolution in the Western Hemisphere and created the first Black republic.","citation":"Lesson: The Haitian Revolution"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"What did the 1825 French indemnity force Haiti to do?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"Pay 150 million gold francs to compensate French slaveholders for their 'lost property,' meaning the freed people"},{"id":"q13b","text":"Return to French rule"},{"id":"q13c","text":"Give up all its sugar"},{"id":"q13d","text":"Nothing at all"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"Threatened with renewed French force, Haiti agreed in 1825 to pay 150 million gold francs, borrowing from French banks to pay French slaveholders for the freedom of Haitian people.","citation":"Lesson: The Debt of Freedom"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"How does the 1825 indemnity connect to Haiti's modern situation?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"The debt, paid through 1947 and worth roughly 21 billion modern dollars, crippled Haiti's economy for over a century"},{"id":"q14b","text":"It made Haiti wealthy"},{"id":"q14c","text":"It had no lasting effect"},{"id":"q14d","text":"It was canceled immediately"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"The double debt, repaid through 1947 and estimated at about 21 billion dollars in modern terms, is a direct line from colonial slavery to Haiti's long economic crippling.","citation":"Lesson: The Debt of Freedom"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"Who were the Maroons?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"Self-liberated enslaved people who built independent communities outside the plantation system"},{"id":"q15b","text":"European plantation owners"},{"id":"q15c","text":"A type of rum"},{"id":"q15d","text":"French soldiers"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"Maroons were people who freed themselves from slavery and built autonomous communities with their own governance, farming, and culture, resisting recapture.","citation":"Lesson: The Maroons"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"Which Maroon community signed a treaty with the British in 1739 and survives today?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"Accompong Town in Jamaica's Cockpit Country"},{"id":"q16b","text":"Palmares in Brazil"},{"id":"q16c","text":"Newport, Rhode Island"},{"id":"q16d","text":"Bordeaux in France"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"Accompong Town in Jamaica signed a 1739 treaty with the British, kept its autonomy, and its descendants maintain their traditions today.","citation":"Lesson: The Maroons"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"Why was rum so profitable for plantation owners?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"Molasses was treated as free waste and enslaved workers did the distilling, so it was high margin on near-zero cost"},{"id":"q17b","text":"Rum was extremely expensive to make"},{"id":"q17c","text":"The government paid them subsidies"},{"id":"q17d","text":"It was sold at a loss"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"Molasses cost nothing as a waste byproduct, enslaved labor cost nothing, and rum sold for real money, so planters extracted the value of both sugar and rum from the same labor.","citation":"Lesson: Waste-Product Economics"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"What did Eric Williams argue in Capitalism and Slavery (1944)?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"Profits from the slave trade and plantation production were a major source of capital for the British Industrial Revolution"},{"id":"q18b","text":"Slavery had no economic effect"},{"id":"q18c","text":"Rum caused the Industrial Revolution alone"},{"id":"q18d","text":"The Caribbean was unprofitable"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"Williams argued that slave-trade and plantation profits significantly funded the British Industrial Revolution, a thesis now broadly though not universally accepted.","citation":"Lesson: Waste-Product Economics"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"In the modern rum supply chain, where does most of a bottle's retail value go?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"To brand marketing, distribution, and retail in the wealthy consuming country, not the producing island"},{"id":"q19b","text":"To the sugarcane farmer"},{"id":"q19c","text":"All to the distillery"},{"id":"q19d","text":"Entirely to taxes"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"For a 40 dollar bottle, the farmer captures around 50 cents to 1 dollar, while the largest shares go to marketing, distribution, and retail in the consuming country.","citation":"Lesson: Who Gets Paid, and Martinique's Answer"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"How does Martinique's rhum agricole AOC function as economic sovereignty?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"A former colony uses the colonizer's own French legal system to protect its product and keep value on the island"},{"id":"q20b","text":"It bans all rum sales"},{"id":"q20c","text":"It gives the rum away for free"},{"id":"q20d","text":"It has no economic effect"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"The 1996 AOC lets Martinique use France's own appellation law to protect its rum, command premium prices, and concentrate value on the island, a form of economic sovereignty.","citation":"Lesson: Who Gets Paid, and Martinique's Answer"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"What is C.L.R. James's central argument in The Black Jacobins?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"Enslaved people were active political agents who made strategy and created new political forms, not passive victims"},{"id":"q21b","text":"The revolution was an accident"},{"id":"q21c","text":"Only France mattered"},{"id":"q21d","text":"Enslaved people had no role"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"James documented the Haitian Revolution to argue that enslaved people were strategic political agents, challenging the colonial assumption that they lacked political capacity.","citation":"Lesson: The Black Jacobins"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"How does the title The Black Jacobins make an argument?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"By naming the enslaved revolutionaries after the most radical French revolutionaries, holding France to its own ideals"},{"id":"q22b","text":"By hiding the subject"},{"id":"q22c","text":"By praising the slaveholders"},{"id":"q22d","text":"The title means nothing"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"Calling them Jacobins links the Haitian revolutionaries to the most radical French revolutionaries, making an argument about liberty and equality before the reader even begins.","citation":"Lesson: The Black Jacobins"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"What is tiki culture?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"A mid-20th century American fantasy mixing Caribbean rum, Polynesian imagery, and a 'tropical paradise' narrative"},{"id":"q23b","text":"A traditional Caribbean religion"},{"id":"q23c","text":"A type of sugarcane"},{"id":"q23d","text":"A French wine region"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"Tiki combined Caribbean rum cocktails, borrowed Polynesian visual imagery, and a fantasy of tropical paradise, layering multiple appropriations.","citation":"Lesson: Tiki and the Tropical Paradise"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"What does the phrase 'tropical paradise' erase?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"The real history of slavery, colonialism, and the complexity of Caribbean and Pacific cultures"},{"id":"q24b","text":"Nothing important"},{"id":"q24c","text":"Only the weather"},{"id":"q24d","text":"The price of rum"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"By suggesting a place of innocent simplicity outside history, 'tropical paradise' erases slavery, colonialism, and the genuine complexity of the cultures it borrows from.","citation":"Lesson: Tiki and the Tropical Paradise"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"What is a counter-narrative?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"A story told to challenge a dominant narrative, like Haitian history against the tiki fantasy"},{"id":"q25b","text":"A type of still"},{"id":"q25c","text":"A tax document"},{"id":"q25d","text":"A grape variety"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"A counter-narrative challenges a dominant story; C.L.R. James's history of agency counters both colonial assumptions and the tiki fantasy that erased the real past.","citation":"Lesson: The Black Jacobins"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"What is dunder in Jamaican rum production?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"Leftover liquid from a previous distillation, added to deepen the funk and complexity"},{"id":"q26b","text":"A type of barrel"},{"id":"q26c","text":"A sugar substitute"},{"id":"q26d","text":"A French legal term"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"Dunder is the leftover liquid from a prior distillation, added back in Jamaica to build microbial complexity tied to a specific distillery.","citation":"Lesson: Different Islands, Different Rums"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"What does 'double extraction' mean in plantation rum economics?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"Planters captured the value of both sugar and rum from the same land and same enslaved labor"},{"id":"q27b","text":"Rum was distilled twice"},{"id":"q27c","text":"Workers were paid double"},{"id":"q27d","text":"Two taxes were charged"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"By turning waste molasses into rum, planters extracted profit from both sugar and rum using the same land and the same enslaved labor force.","citation":"Lesson: Waste-Product Economics"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"How does rum producers' share of retail value compare to coffee farmers'?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"Rum producers capture about 12 to 25 percent, better than coffee farmers' roughly 6 to 10 percent, but still extractive"},{"id":"q28b","text":"They capture 100 percent"},{"id":"q28c","text":"They capture nothing"},{"id":"q28d","text":"Exactly the same as coffee"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"Caribbean rum producers keep about 12 to 25 percent of retail, better than Ethiopian coffee farmers' 6 to 10 percent, but the chain still moves most value to consuming nations.","citation":"Lesson: Who Gets Paid, and Martinique's Answer"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"What does it mean to treat enslaved people as agents in historical writing?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"Recognizing that they made choices, strategies, and decisions that drove historical change"},{"id":"q29b","text":"Treating them only as property"},{"id":"q29c","text":"Ignoring them entirely"},{"id":"q29d","text":"Assuming they had no role"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"Agency means the capacity to act and choose; C.L.R. James treated enslaved people as political agents whose decisions shaped the Haitian Revolution.","citation":"Lesson: The Black Jacobins"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"Why is this episode called Sweet Poison?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"Rum is the sweet spirit of sugar, but its history is bound to the cruelty of the slave trade"},{"id":"q30b","text":"Rum is literally poisonous"},{"id":"q30c","text":"It is made from poison plants"},{"id":"q30d","text":"The name has no meaning"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"Rum is the spirit of sugar, and to tell its history honestly is to hold both the craft and the cruelty of slavery at once, the sweetness and the poison.","citation":"Lesson: Sweet Poison (intro)"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"What is the Middle Passage?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"The forced Atlantic crossing carrying enslaved Africans to the Americas, the second leg of the trade"},{"id":"q31b","text":"A rum aging method"},{"id":"q31c","text":"A Caribbean island"},{"id":"q31d","text":"A type of still"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"The Middle Passage was the brutal second leg of the triangular trade, the forced ocean crossing on which roughly 1.8 million of about 12.5 million Africans died.","citation":"Lesson: The Triangular Trade"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"Which island is considered the birthplace of rum?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"Barbados, where a balanced, elegant style developed on coral limestone"},{"id":"q32b","text":"Cuba"},{"id":"q32c","text":"Iceland"},{"id":"q32d","text":"Sicily"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"Barbados, on coral limestone soil, is the birthplace of rum and known for a balanced, elegant, approachable style.","citation":"Lesson: Different Islands, Different Rums"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"What is cultural appropriation, as seen in tiki culture?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"Taking cultural elements from a marginalized group without permission or understanding"},{"id":"q33b","text":"Studying a culture respectfully"},{"id":"q33c","text":"A type of trade tax"},{"id":"q33d","text":"Sharing your own culture"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"Tiki took Polynesian sacred figures as decoration and Caribbean rum without context, an example of appropriation that erased real histories.","citation":"Lesson: Tiki and the Tropical Paradise"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"What does full cost accounting reveal about the triangular trade?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"Counting human suffering and long-term harm produces a radically different assessment than the operators' profit ledgers"},{"id":"q34b","text":"The trade was a clear public good"},{"id":"q34c","text":"There were no costs at all"},{"id":"q34d","text":"Only money mattered"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"Colonial ledgers recorded only the operators' profit and loss; counting human welfare, destroyed communities, and underdevelopment gives a very different picture of profitability.","citation":"Lesson: Waste-Product Economics"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"What did Maroon communities build?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"Autonomous governance, independent farming, preserved African culture, and the military skill to resist recapture"},{"id":"q35b","text":"Sugar plantations"},{"id":"q35c","text":"Slave-trading ports"},{"id":"q35d","text":"Nothing lasting"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"Maroon communities built self-governing societies with their own farming economies, preserved African traditions, and the military knowledge to defend their freedom.","citation":"Lesson: The Maroons"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"Why is rum a clear example of terroir in spirits?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"Each island's climate, soil, still, and tradition leave a distinct fingerprint on its rum"},{"id":"q36b","text":"All rum tastes identical"},{"id":"q36c","text":"Rum has no flavor"},{"id":"q36d","text":"Rum is made only in one place"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"From funky Jamaica to grassy Martinique agricole to dark Demerara Guyana, the raw material, still, climate, and local tradition give each island's rum its own character.","citation":"Lesson: Different Islands, Different Rums"}]}
